notes: "ROO-bin". NVIDIA GPU generation named after dark-matter astronomer Vera Rubin; paired with the Vera CPU, successor to Blackwell.

source_label: MIT Technology Review — Yann LeCun’s AMI Labs ("ami", French for friend)
notes: Two names share the letters: AWS’s Amazon Machine Image is read letter-by-letter A-M-I (with "ah-mee" a common informal reading), while Yann LeCun’s AMI Labs is creator-clarified as "ah-MEE" — French for "friend".

source_label: Baidu Baike (EN) — Taalas, name from Hindi for locksmith
notes: Name derives from Hindi taalaa/taalasaaz ('lock'/'locksmith'), hence stressed long ah vowels, not 'TAY-las'.
OpenAI/Broadcom inference processor; Spanish loanword with j = /h/ and ñ = /nj/.
MLX-based Apple Silicon LLM inference server; said as "oh" plus the letters M-L-X, like its parent framework.
Microsoft Azure AI accelerator named for the Greek Pleiad; trade press ("My, oh Maia") leans MY-ah while the classical English reading is MAY-uh.
Microsoft's topological quantum chip line named after physicist Ettore Majorana; English usage is my-uh-RAH-nuh — the Italian j is a glide, never /dʒ/.

source_label: Microsoft AI — seven new MAI models (Build 2026)
notes: Microsoft AI's in-house model family (MAI-Thinking-1, MAI-Voice-2, ...); read letter-by-letter like the "AI" it contains — Microsoft has published no official pronunciation.
d-Matrix's in-memory-compute inference accelerator (also the PC-hardware brand); American dictionaries stress the first syllable — KOR-sair, not the French-style cor-SAIR.
Portmanteau of light + httpd; the official site states it is pronounced "lighty".
Self-hosted photo/video backup server; creator Alex Tran: "It is pronounced as 'image'", though much of the community says IMM-itch.
Official FAQ: "dee-BEE-zee-uhm" — say "DBs-ium" fast; from DBs + the periodic-table -ium suffix.
Apache dataflow tool from NSA's "NiagaraFiles"; the official FAQ states "nye fye" is the preferred pronunciation.
CNCF application life-cycle orchestration project (archived Sept 2025); official glossary states "Keptn, pronounced captain."

source_label: Tyk GitHub issue #195 — "How do you pronounce tyk?"
notes: Open-source Go API gateway; creator Martin Buhr confirmed on GitHub it rhymes with "Mike" — /taɪk/.
FAQ: usually "ZAP-ian" like the word zap; "ZAY-pian" is a popular variant (name mangles "sapient").

source_label: Eason Tech Talk — Kueue quick start ("pronounced like queue")
notes: Kubernetes-native job-queueing controller (kubernetes-sigs); the K-wordplay name is read exactly like "queue" — one syllable, kyoo.

Cloud host (acquired by Akamai in 2022; now Akamai Connected Cloud), from "Linux node"; the official @linode account clarified "We pronounce it Lih-node - 'lin' as in Linux!", though LIE-node remains a common mispronunciation.
Linux Foundation fork of HashiCorp Vault named after baozi, the steamed bun (per GitHub discussion #34) — "bao" rhymes with "cow", not "bay-oh".
Wheeler's database change-management tool ("SQL changes with Git stuck in the middle"); pronounced "skitch", not "skwitch".
Google's Java dependency-injection framework; the README opens with "Guice (pronounced 'juice')".
Java/Spring server-side template engine; the official FAQ spells it out as /ˈtaɪmˌlɪːf/ — th sounds like plain t, as in the herb thyme (TIME-leaf).

source_label: Kubernetes Podcast #081 — creators on the name (French "vitesse")
notes: CNCF-graduated MySQL sharding system from YouTube; named after French "vitesse" (speed), so stress falls on the second syllable.
Lightweight libc behind Alpine Linux images; official site says "musl, pronounced like the word 'mussel' or 'muscle'".
Promise-based HTTP client among npm's most-downloaded packages; from Greek axios ("worthy") with no official ruling — top-voted community reading is "AK-see-ohs".

Java mocking framework whose name is a maintainer-confirmed pun on mock + mojito; a maintainer answered issue #889 with mock-based /mɑˈkiːtoʊ/ ("mah-KEE-toh"), while creator Szczepan Faber told the mailing list to "go ahead and pronounce it the mojito way" ("moh-KEE-toh"), so the split is genuine.
Python-powered cross-platform shell; the official homepage states it "sounds like 'consh'".
CPython compiled to WebAssembly for running Python in the browser; maintainers rth and hoodmane gave the IPA ˈpɑɪ.ə.ˌdɑɪd (Py + Iodide — rhymes with iodide).
Cross-platform language compiling to JS, C++, JVM and more; early official docs said "hex" while English speakers naturally say "hacks" — no creator ruling, both survive at conferences.
Veteran cross-platform C++ GUI toolkit; the official docs open with 'The Fast Light Tool Kit ("FLTK", pronounced "fulltick")'.
POSIX compatibility layer for Windows (Cygnus + Windows); Wikipedia gives /ˈsɪɡwɪn/ SIG-win, confirmed by a Cygwin developer on the project mailing list.

source_label: Nagios official FAQ F0003 (archived) — Ethan Galstad: "nah-ghee-ose"
notes: Veteran open-source infrastructure monitoring system (NetSaint 1999, renamed 2002); creator Ethan Galstad's official FAQ spells it out as "nah-ghee-ose" — "Nah" plus the start of "geese" plus the end of "verbose" — so the g is hard, never "NAY-jee-os", and Wikipedia records the same /ˈnɑːɡiːoʊs/.
